name: Ph.D. in Mathematical Sciences

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 20 referenced courses span three coherent doctoral tracks that build with real depth (deepestPrereqChain 3): a pure-analysis spine (MATH 645 Analysis I to MATH 745 Analysis II and MATH 768 measure-theoretic Probability Theory, plus MATH 656 Complex Variables), an applied-math track (MATH 613 Modeling to MATH 689 Advanced Applied Math II: ODEs, with MATH 614 Numerical Methods I and MATH 651), and a full statistics sequence (MATH 661/662 to MATH 665 Inference, feeding MATH 644 Regression, MATH 698 Sampling, MATH 699 Design of Experiments). Progression is well ordered; the main weakness is that no plan-of-study groups were parsed (requirementGroups 0), so qualifying-exam structure and the coursework/research split are not legible.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish an explicit plan of study that groups courses into breadth areas (analysis, applied mathematics, statistics) and shows the qualifying-exam, coursework, and dissertation-research stages.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is healthy and reaches a sensible doctoral depth of 3 (e.g., MATH 546 to MATH 645 to MATH 745/768 and to MATH 656), with statistics gated cleanly on MATH 662 to MATH 665 to the methods courses. Only one dangling reference exists (missingCourseCount 1, MATH 790A), and it is a dissertation-research registration course rather than a substantive gap.
dimensions.suggestion: Add MATH 790A (dissertation research) to the catalog so the last dangling reference resolves, and confirm each research course (725, 792B) is listed.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or doctoral educational objectives appear in the data; for a Ph.D. the expected competencies (breadth mastery, qualifying-exam standards, original-research capacity) are left implicit.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish doctoral learning outcomes covering breadth-area mastery, qualifying/candidacy expectations, and dissertation research and teaching competencies.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits and creditReconcileDelta are both null and no plan groups were parsed, so the coursework-versus-research credit split is not visible; for a Ph.D. this total is inherently research-dominated and less fixed than for a taught degree, so the opacity is a transparency note rather than a hard defect. The course mix (coursework plus MATH 725 Independent Study, 791 Seminar, 792B Pre-Doctoral Research) implies a conventional coursework-then-research balance.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total post-baccalaureate credit requirement and the minimum coursework versus dissertation-research credits so prospective students can see the balance.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Course descriptions are present and specific for all sampled courses and titles clearly signal the analysis, applied, and statistics tracks, but the page shows no plan of study, no stated credit total, and no labeled stages, so the reader must infer the doctoral structure from prerequisites alone.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a structured plan-of-study table with labeled breadth areas and stages plus a credit summary to complement the strong course descriptions.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The curriculum reflects strong contemporary computational and data-oriented mathematics: numerical methods (MATH 614), modeling (MATH 613), and a deep applied-statistics stack (regression, design of experiments, sampling, statistical inference, measure-theoretic probability). It is comparatively light on optimization, machine/statistical learning, and applied stochastic processes, which are now central to both academic and industrial mathematical-sciences research.
dimensions.suggestion: Add or require a graduate course in optimization and one in statistical/machine learning so doctoral students match the computational-data frontier employers and funders now expect.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are substantive and specific, articulating each course's scope (e.g., MATH 768 as a measure-theoretic axiomatic probability course, MATH 614 emphasizing accuracy and rigor in scientific computation) rather than placeholder text; excerpts are truncated but clearly full entries exist.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated as explicit course codes with sensible sequencing and departmental-approval alternatives (e.g., MATH 665 requires MATH 662; MATH 745 requires MATH 645); only MATH 790A dangles and a few gates read 'or departmental approval' rather than a hard course.

missingTopics.rationale: The applied and numerical track (MATH 613/614/651/689) has no dedicated optimization course, yet optimization underpins modern applied mathematics, operations research, and machine learning.

Despite a strong classical-statistics stack (MATH 644/665/698/699), no course covers high-dimensional or statistical learning, a core competency for contemporary mathematical-sciences Ph.D. graduates.
MATH 768 is measure-theoretic probability but there is no applied stochastic-processes, Markov-chain, or stochastic-differential-equations course to support modeling and simulation.
MATH 689 covers boundary-value ODEs but no dedicated partial-differential-equations or finite-element/numerical-PDE course appears, a gap for an applied-mathematics doctorate.
The statistics track stops at frequentist regression/inference; MCMC, bootstrap, and Bayesian computation are absent and are now standard doctoral tools.

emergingTrends.trend: Scientific machine learning and physics-informed models
emergingTrends.rationale: Data-driven scientific computing (e.g., physics-informed neural networks) is reshaping applied mathematics; the MATH 613/614/689 sequence could add a scientific-ML component.
UQ links numerical methods and probability and is central to modern modeling; it would naturally extend MATH 614 and the probability courses.
Geometry- and topology-driven data methods are a fast-growing research frontier that the analysis and statistics tracks are well positioned to support.
MCMC and probabilistic modeling now dominate applied statistical research; adding them modernizes the MATH 662/665 sequence.
Large-scale simulation increasingly relies on parallel/GPU computing, an extension the numerical-methods track (MATH 614) should reflect.

topSuggestions: Publish a plan of study with breadth areas, qualifying-exam structure, and the coursework-versus-dissertation credit split, replacing the null statedTotalCredits.
State doctoral learning outcomes (breadth mastery, candidacy standards, original-research and teaching competencies).
Add graduate optimization and statistical/machine-learning courses to close the biggest currency gap versus the research frontier.
Introduce applied stochastic processes/SDEs and a numerical/advanced PDE course to round out the applied-mathematics track.
Resolve the dangling MATH 790A dissertation-research reference in the catalog.
